speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 4th, 1996. We are interviewing Mr. Fred Findling. The interviewer is Sharon Alterman. We are conducting our interview in Royal Oak, Michigan, USA, and the language of the interview is English. My name is Sharon Alterman. I'm interviewing Mr. Fred Findling in Royal Oak, Michigan, USA, and today's date is October 4th, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: First time I [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Please give me your name at birth and please spell it for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth, it was Siegfried Findling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how is that spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-I-E-G-F-R-I-E-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cologne, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And please spell that also.

speaker: interviewee
text: C-O-L-O-G-N-E, Germany-- G-E-R-M-A-N-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 4, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your age at present?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be 66 on December 4th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Findling, for being part of the Shoah project. We're going to start by talking about your family and your family history. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David Wolfe Findling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And please spell that for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: David, D-A-V-I-D, and Wolf, W-O-L-F, and Findling, F-I-N-D-L-I-N-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Fryzstag.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: F-R-Y-Z-S-T-A-G. And that's-- we used to say near Jaslov in Poland. It's a small village in the western part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be the closest city that that would be near?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say there is another name called [INAUDIBLE], but I would say Jaslov is what I'm familiar with.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how it that spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-A-S-L-O-V. That's probably the German spelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, what was her name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Etla, E-T-L-A, Gottesdiener, G-O-T-T-E-S-D-I-E-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: July 22, 1905. And did you ask my father's date of birth? David Wolf Findling was born March 6, 1901.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, Poland, W-A-R-S-A-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was born in Fryzstag and you had grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father's grandparents I never knew. I understand that they also lived in Fryzstag. And my father came from a large family and my grandfather is supposed to have been a redhead and there were a lot of redheads in the Findling family in Fryzstag. And I never met any of my father's family. When he came to Germany, he communicated to his family in Fryzstag, but nobody ever came from Fryzstag to visit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I never met any of my uncles or aunts or my grandfather on my father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your maternal grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maternal grandparents, we do have more of a history. I have a family tree that goes back a number of years. And in Cologne, Germany, my father's-- my mother's younger brother lived with us and his name was at that time Herman Gottesdiener. And Herman, H-E-R-M-A-N, Gottesdiener, the same spelling as my mother. He later changed his name in life to Charles Gordon, C-H-A-R-L-E-S, Gordon, G-O-R-D-O-N. That's a far cry from Herman Gottesdiener, but that's a long story. In any event, he was single and lived with us in Cologne, Germany. I know that I-- my mother also had older sisters, three older sisters. And I never met them, but they had children. And in my story, one or two of them you will know about later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when did your father come to Cologne?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I understand, he came to Cologne some time after World War I and my mother did that also, in the early '20s from Fryzstag and my mother from Warsaw. And they came to Cologne and they met in Cologne.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they marry?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have-- there-- in my family, there-- there were five. Joseph Findling is the oldest. He was born June 21st, 1928. Then the next is my sister, Fanny. Should I spell that? F-A-N-N-Y. And she was born November 3, 1929. I am the third and I was born December 4, 1930. Then my younger brother, Martin, M-A-R-T-I-N, he was born August 8, 1932. And then the youngest is Regina. She was born May 8th, 1937. They are all still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to know-- we'd like to know-- about your home and the part of Cologne that you lived in. Can you tell us about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My earliest recollection was we lived in-- on a street called Houser-- Hosengasse. Hosengasse is like a small avenue or a small street and like an alley. Hosen, H-O-S-E-N, and that means pants. So we lived in the little street called the Pants Street, Hosengasse. Then when I was about six and a half, seven years old, we moved to 50 Alexianer Strasse. And that's spelled A-L-E-X- I-A-N-E-R Strasse in Cologne, not far from the Cologne Cathedral that I know as Kolm Dom. And it was not far from a square called Neumark, N-E-U-M-A-R-K. I think that's the spelling. And we lived there until I left Cologne, Germany in 1938-- at the end of 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to your first home. And can you describe the community and a little bit about the physical home itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one on Hosengasse?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We lived across from a-- what we would call a deaf and dumb place. It was an apartment, old apartment. We didn't have any public toilet. We had to go in a separate area for public toilets. It was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --a small apartment. And we call it-- all four of us, there were four at that time before my younger sister, Reggie, was born-- we all slept in one bed, two facing one way and two facing the other way. And of course, if anyone wet to bed, there would be accusatory fingers as to who was responsible. And-- but that was our life and we were accustomed to that. And my father and mother had their own bedroom. So we had two bedrooms, one for all four children and one for my mother and father. The stove that we had was one of those old fashioned kind, where they're lit by coal or wood. And they had these metal circular rings that were on top of the stove and you would remove as many as you'd need in order to make the fire hotter. If you wanted to make the fire low, you would just add on more of these rings around the circle until it was completely closed. That was the way we did our cooking. As far as food is concerned, my understanding was my parents were kosher so that he-- my father would go out and buy live chickens and we'd keep it in the house under the bed until they could be taken to a-- a [INAUDIBLE], I think it's called, who would slaughter the chicken and we would have kosher meat. But that was the way we ate. That was my recollection of Hosengasse. My recollections at that period of time of a child were that I had a normal family life in the house. Outside the house, it was not normal. We knew from the earliest age that there was discrimination against the Jews in Germany. And we knew from an early age how to walk in the streets to avoid trouble and what to do in order to not get ourselves hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at my earliest age, I already knew that I was different than most Germans-- or considered different than most Germans. Let's talk a little about your life in the home. You said that that was normal and you mentioned that you were a religious family. Can you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm not sure about religious. My father was kosher. He belonged to a little synagogue called the [GERMAN]. He was Polish national, never became a German. And the friends and acquaintances were mostly Polish people that he knew. And he belonged to a synagogue, a little synagogue. And of course, on Saturday he could not take the street car. He'd have to walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sometimes it would be a great distance to walk to the synagogue. So he-- my father was not that observant. He would once in a while catch the streetcar to go to [GERMAN]. And of course, he was ostracized when he was discovered as having taken the streetcar on Shabbat or Yom Kippur or Rosh Hashanah. But my recollection of-- of-- of those years is that my father was observant, but not necessarily religious. That was the way of the Jewish people in Cologne. What else can I add? How were the holidays, the yomtevs, observed in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had Shabbat candles, we had challah, and we had the wine. And it took me a long time to figure out what was the language spoken at home. And I-- we must have spoken Yiddish because I do understand Yiddish and I could not have learned unless I spoke it at home. I have no awareness of having spoken Yiddish because I spoke German. But I do know from my earliest times that I could converse in Yiddish and understand Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think that Yiddish was spoken in the home, if not on a regular basis at least on a frequent basis. And we had the usual trappings of a Jewish family. We had the-- the pushke, which we are all familiar with. That's where you put the money. And my family always kept the-- a menorah. We always had the-- the trappings of a Jewish home.

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned Hebrew at an early age, except that the Hebrew learned was not to understand but was mostly for to-- to be able to perform the rituals, to say the [HEBREW] the-- the prayers. But we were never taught Hebrew with a sense of being able to learn the language. It was only for the purposes of davening, which means to pray. And I have never learned Hebrew. Although I can read and daven very fluently, I have never learned to understand what I am reading to this day. That's a sad commentary [BACKGROUND NOISE] of our life, but that was the way the Jewish people were in those days. And Hebrew was not taught in the sense of enlightenment. It was taught with a cat of nine tails, we call it. [LAUGHS] When we went to cheder in those days, we-- we could see in the corner of our eye that there was that-- that [YIDDISH]. That's the Yiddish word for-- for whip. And it actually was a cat of nine tails. It had nine leather throngs on it. And my older brother used to say, and when the teacher wasn't looking, you'd get a razor blade and cut off one at a time, hoping that over a period of time, there wouldn't be many left. But the point was that the [YIDDISH] was used to teach you how to learn Hebrew. It was more or less enforced on you with the crack of a whip. But that was the way Hebrew was taught in school during that period of time. From my earliest recollections in Cologne, we could never mingle with Germans. We always mingled with the people-- Jewish people. And we lived in an area which was predominantly Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like, just the physical nature of that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was what you would probably imagine to be similar to what Flatbush was like in the '40s if you remember or have any idea of Flatbush, Lower East Side New York. That was a similar type of lifestyle-- pushcarts up the streets, people thronging about, a lot of Jewish people-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --going back and forth. But not too many Germans amongst them. And if the Germans did come, of course, and they came only for purposes of making trouble, but it was not that noticeable to us because it wasn't-- the Jewish people did not-- in Cologne, Germany wear the payoses and the-- the black clothing, black hats. They didn't wear yarmulkes in public. But you knew that from the people that you were acquainted with that they were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were more modern? In dress.

speaker: interviewee
text: They would be-- I would say that would be more modern in dress, although the Germans always said in those days that you could tell a Jew by his nose. And I always looked in the mirror and I could never figure out how he could tell if I was Jewish by looking at my nose. But evidently, the Jew-- the Jews were singled out as being recognizable because they had a bump in their nose. And Germans, you could-- would go down the street and identify you and say, that a-- that's a Juden by his nose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- we became conscious of the fact that we were singled out as different from the others and that we were not the same and were not allowed to do the same things. We-- I don't remember if I ever went to a public school. I think I went there-- no, I don't-- I never went. I went to kindergarten that was a Jewish kindergarten. And then I knew I was not allowed to go in public school, so I went to a Jewish school, predominantly for Jewish people because they would not allow children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I was old enough to go to school, they would not allow Jewish children to go to public schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall a Seder in your home or any religious observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We did observe the holidays. We observed-- I-- I do recall that when we ate for Sukkot, we would go to the roof of the apartment. And there-- there was sort of a-- an enclosed space like a shed on the apartment roof and we would use that for eating. And we would go up there and it would be decorated. And the men could only eat, the women had to serve the food. And we went up there to eat. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --I remember that the Passover dinners were long and drawn out. It was nothing like the way we do it now. It was all in Hebrew, nothing in German, nothing in Yiddish-- just all Hebrew. And you would sit there endlessly. And as a young child, I was bored stiff to the constant davening that was going on. Nobody knew what was going on or what they were saying, but it was ritual. And I-- in my-- in my earliest times, I said, if I ever do this, it's going to be done so everybody will understand. But the Jews in those days, when they had Passover meals, there was a-- it was a long, drawn out affair. The children hated it [LAUGHS] because you couldn't understand anything that was going on. The food was great when you finally got around to eating it. But in terms of what-- understanding what was going on around you, nobody did. Nobody knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to hear a little bit about your parents, what kinds of people they were, what your recollections were.

speaker: interviewee
text: My recollection of my father-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --you have to remember that I was eight years old when I last saw my father. But what I do recollect from him is that he was not an educated man and did not have any skills. He was an itinerant worker. Itinerant worker means he held jobs every once in a while. And I found out later in life that he had worked part time at a lumber yard and half the time he was on the public dole, that is on charity. And when-- even when he was working, he didn't make much money and we always had to have assistance. And assistance like, for instance, the shoes would be handed down from older child to younger child. When pairs of shoes were done, he would-- my father would go and get heels and he'd put the heels on himself on the shoes to repair them. My father never owned a car. I don't think he knew how to drive, didn't own a motorcycle. And he had a pushcart, one of these wooden pushcarts. And I think his greatest-- his greatest material acquisition was a bicycle. And before I left, he finally got himself a bicycle. So that in terms of wealth, we were probably very, very poor. We had food on the table, we were well-dressed, my mother saw to that. And we were given a lot of love, we were given warmth, we were given affection. But material things we didn't have. But I-- I didn't know any better anyways. So-- but my father in terms of his background, I don't recollect my father as ever having any culture education. I do recollect sadly that my father was perhaps not a very sophisticated man. He was a simple man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I do remember that when I was young, it was common among all the men during the day when they had to urinate, they would go against the wall and urinate against the wall. And my father, when I was real young, insisted that I urinate in the gutter. And I would always ask him, when will I be able to be old enough to do it against the wall? So those are remembrances that I had. But it was quite customary for men in those days to go against any wall to urinate.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't see that nowadays, but in-- that was the style. The other thing is that my father, at times, never used a handkerchief to blow his nose. He's use his-- use his fingers. And I-- when I first saw him do that, or when I first recollected him doing that, even at my younger age, I thought it was a little imprudent, perhaps. And I knew that I have never done that in my life. But whether or not this was part of his upbringing or whether or not that this was the convention of the time, these are things that I do recollect of my father at an early life. My mother I have also fond memories, but not-- my memories of my mother are of a woman that was under a lot of pressure and she had at times fainting spells. And whether or not they were epileptic seizures or not, I don't know. I've asked members of my family. I've asked my uncle, my mother's younger brother, Herman Gottesdiener, now Charles Gordon, who passed away last year, whether or not she did have epileptic fits. And he said no, he thought it was mostly nervousness. So my mother was, perhaps because the pressures of the time, having five children, living in a small-- small apartment, not having any amenities, and perhaps being responsible for the upbringing of five children, was a nervous woman and at times had nervous spells. I know that she was a warm, affectionate woman. And I know that she was a caring woman. And I know that she loved all of us dearly. In terms of her background, she also was not a very educated woman. And whatever knowledge I've acquired, if I have-- whatever I have acquired in my life has not been from anything that I can attribute to my mother or father in terms of exposure to culture, art, in terms of music. These things I have acquired since, on my own, from my own discoveries. Although in Germany, even though it was a horrible place to live, it still had a historical tradition of music and art. And I was exposed to music at a very early age, good music.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: From radio, from phonographs. And-- and that type of exposure. From bands, the band playing in-- music. I would hear marches by von Suppe or other composers and I would hear classical music being played-- Brahms, Schubert, lot of Schubert Lieders. So I had an exposure to classical music in early life. My father knew only material wealthy and he had a radio. We had a radio in the house. We didn't have a phonograph. And that's about the extent of the things that I can remember about my father. I do-- I do remember my father was warm and affectionate to me. I have a recollection walking with him once. I must have been about three or four. And I remember I was tired, I couldn't walk anymore and he picked me up and put me on his shoulder. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --that-- that to me, I think, is a sign that he didn't force me to walk, he helped me when I was tired. And that is a fond memory I have of him. I don't-- my parents were not disciplinarians. I never recollect ever getting spanked, you know. I don't have any knowledge or background of ever being hit. I do remember my mother telling my father, give him a whipping, and my father taking his belt and then taking me in the bedroom and then putting the belt together and cracking it and making noise and then yelling at me and say, I give him a good whipping. And my mother saying, OK, good. Don't-- don't overdo it. You know, just enough, a little bit, not too much. And that that to me is a recollection-- fond recollection that I have that my father never had any mean streak in him and that my mother wanted my father to do something but not really to be violent. And I-- I used that technique also with my children when they grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a good one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had an uncle in your home, too, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. My Uncle Charles, I'll call him Charles Gordon now, because he changed his name when he finally got to Great Britain. He was in his late 20s when he lived with us. He lived in Alexianer Strasse with us, but he had his own separate room. And when I say separate room, the apartment as you left the exit had is another room across the hallway. And it's one room and he stayed in there and he ate his meals with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you could say he lived with us. And he stayed there for several years, until I left. And then in October 1938, when my father and my uncle were deported to Poland, he finally got to England. And I'll tell that story a little later. But he was what you call a--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --he didn't really have any education or skills, either. He sort of traded in clothing and also he was a baker at one time. But he also was very warm and affectionate. He was my mother's younger brother and he-- I had very fond memories of him. I used to be a sickly child, and when I say sickly I used to-- I used to get all kinds of illnesses when I was very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause here. This is the end of tape number one.
